reasoning: The ECB generally maintains a path of Quantitative Tightening in 2025, actively shrinking its balance sheet through the run-offs of its APP and PEPP portfolios. As a result, the structural trend for the balance sheet is downward. Over a 1-week horizon (from late October to November 2, 2025), normal weekly volatility—driven by changes in Main Refinancing Operations (MROs) and other fluctuating autonomous factors—often causes random short-term deviations, leaving a sizable chance of a slight increase compared to the reference date. Over a 1-month and especially 3-month horizon (January 2026), the cumulative effects of the negative QT drift usually dominate, which typically reduces the probability of a higher balance sheet. However, longer timeframes also bring higher fundamental uncertainty—such as large positive year-end revaluations of gold/FX reserves or unexpected liquidity injections due to unforeseen market stresses. Following the instruction to increase my uncertainty towards 0.5 as the horizon expands, I have structured my probabilities to reflect significant short-term noise, with uncertainty rising with the forecast horizon.
